From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British actress (1929–2010)

Not to be confused with [Gene Simmons](/wiki/Gene_Simmons "Gene Simmons") or [Jen Simmons](/wiki/Jen_Simmons "Jen Simmons").

**Jean Merilyn Simmons** (31 January 1929 – 22 January 2010) was a British actress and singer.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) One of [J. Arthur Rank](/wiki/J._Arthur_Rank "J. Arthur Rank")'s "well-spoken young starlets", she appeared predominantly in films, beginning with [those made in Britain](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_Kingdom "Cinema of the United Kingdom") during and after the Second World War, followed mainly by [Hollywood films](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") from 1950 onwards.[[3]](#cite_note-NYTObit-3)

Simmons was nominated for the [Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Supporting_Actress "Academy Award for Best Supporting Actress") for *[Hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(1948_film) "Hamlet (1948 film)")* (1948), and won a [Golden Globe Award for Best Actress](/wiki/Golden_Globe_Award_for_Best_Actress_in_a_Motion_Picture_%E2%80%93_Musical_or_Comedy "Golden Globe Award for Best Actress in a Motion Picture – Musical or Comedy") for *[Guys and Dolls](/wiki/Guys_and_Dolls_(film) "Guys and Dolls (film)")* (1955). Among her other films were *[Great Expectations](/wiki/Great_Expectations_(1946_film) "Great Expectations (1946 film)")* (1946), *[Black Narcissus](/wiki/Black_Narcissus "Black Narcissus")* (1947), *[The Blue Lagoon](/wiki/The_Blue_Lagoon_(1949_film) "The Blue Lagoon (1949 film)")* (1949), *[So Long at the Fair](/wiki/So_Long_at_the_Fair "So Long at the Fair")* (1950), *[Angel Face](/wiki/Angel_Face_(1953_film) "Angel Face (1953 film)")* (1953), *[Young Bess](/wiki/Young_Bess "Young Bess")* (1953), *[The Robe](/wiki/The_Robe_(film) "The Robe (film)")* (1953), *[The Big Country](/wiki/The_Big_Country "The Big Country")* (1958), *[Elmer Gantry](/wiki/Elmer_Gantry_(film) "Elmer Gantry (film)")* (1960), *[Spartacus](/wiki/Spartacus_(film) "Spartacus (film)")* (1960), and *[The Happy Ending](/wiki/The_Happy_Ending "The Happy Ending")* (1969), for which she was nominated for the [Academy Award for Best Actress](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actress "Academy Award for Best Actress"). She also won an [Emmy Award](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award") for the miniseries *[The Thorn Birds](/wiki/The_Thorn_Birds_(miniseries) "The Thorn Birds (miniseries)")* (1983).

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Simmons&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Simmons was born on 31 January 1929, in [Islington](/wiki/Islington "Islington"), London,[[4]](#cite_note-4) to [Charles Simmons](/wiki/Charles_Simmons_(gymnast) "Charles Simmons (gymnast)"), a physical education teacher,[[5]](#cite_note-5) and his wife, Winifred Ada (née Loveland). Jean was the youngest of four children, with siblings Lorna, Harold, and Edna. She began acting at the age of 14.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

During the Second World War, the Simmons family was evacuated to [Winscombe](/wiki/Winscombe "Winscombe"), [Somerset](/wiki/Somerset "Somerset").[[7]](#cite_note-picturegoer-7) Her father, a bronze medalist in [gymnastics at the 1912 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Gymnastics_at_the_1912_Summer_Olympics "Gymnastics at the 1912 Summer Olympics"), taught briefly at [Sidcot School](/wiki/Sidcot_School "Sidcot School"), and sometime during this period, Simmons followed her eldest sister onto the village stage and sang popular songs such as "[Daddy Wouldn't Buy Me a Bow Wow](/wiki/Daddy_Wouldn%27t_Buy_Me_a_Bow_Wow "Daddy Wouldn't Buy Me a Bow Wow")". At this point, her ambition was to be an [acrobatic dancer](/wiki/Acrobatic_dance "Acrobatic dance").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Simmons&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Early films

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Simmons&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early films")]

On her return to London from Somerset, Simmons enrolled at the [Aida Foster School of Dance](/wiki/Aida_Foster_stage_school "Aida Foster stage school"). She was spotted by director [Val Guest](/wiki/Val_Guest "Val Guest"), who cast her in a large role as [Margaret Lockwood](/wiki/Margaret_Lockwood "Margaret Lockwood")'s sister in *[Give Us the Moon](/wiki/Give_Us_the_Moon "Give Us the Moon")* (1944).[[9]](#cite_note-9)
Small roles in several other films followed, including *[Mr. Emmanuel](/wiki/Mr._Emmanuel "Mr. Emmanuel")* (1944), *[Kiss the Bride Goodbye](/wiki/Kiss_the_Bride_Goodbye "Kiss the Bride Goodbye")* (1945), *[Meet Sexton Blake](/wiki/Meet_Sexton_Blake "Meet Sexton Blake")* (1945), and the popular *[The Way to the Stars](/wiki/The_Way_to_the_Stars "The Way to the Stars")* (1945), as well as the short *[Sports Day](/wiki/Sports_Day_(film) "Sports Day (film)")* (1945).

Simmons had a small part as a harpist in the high-profile *[Caesar and Cleopatra](/wiki/Caesar_and_Cleopatra_(film) "Caesar and Cleopatra (film)")* (1945), produced by [Gabriel Pascal](/wiki/Gabriel_Pascal "Gabriel Pascal"), starring [Vivien Leigh](/wiki/Vivien_Leigh "Vivien Leigh"), and co-starring Simmons's future husband [Stewart Granger](/wiki/Stewart_Granger "Stewart Granger").

### Stardom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Simmons&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Stardom")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Jean_Simmons_1948_crop.jpg/250px-Jean_Simmons_1948_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jean_Simmons_1948_crop.jpg)

Simmons at the [1948 Academy Awards](/wiki/20th_Academy_Awards "20th Academy Awards"), where she received her first [Oscar](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards") nomination

Simmons was cast as the young Estella in [David Lean](/wiki/David_Lean "David Lean")'s version of *[Great Expectations](/wiki/Great_Expectations_(1946_film) "Great Expectations (1946 film)")* (1946). The film was the third-most-popular at the British box office in 1947, Simmons received excellent reviews,[[10]](#cite_note-10) and achieved stardom in the UK.

The experience of working on *Great Expectations* caused her to pursue an acting career more seriously:

> I thought acting was just a lark, meeting all those exciting movie stars, and getting £5 a day which was lovely because we needed the money. But I figured I'd just go off and get married and have children like my mother. It was working with David Lean that convinced me to go on.[[11]](#cite_note-weekly-11)

Simmons had support roles in *[Hungry Hill](/wiki/Hungry_Hill_(film) "Hungry Hill (film)")* (1947) with Margaret Lockwood and the [Powell-Pressburger](/wiki/Powell_and_Pressburger "Powell and Pressburger") film *[Black Narcissus](/wiki/Black_Narcissus "Black Narcissus")* (1947), playing an Indian woman in the latter alongside [Sabu](/wiki/Sabu_Dastagir "Sabu Dastagir").[[12]](#cite_note-reelclassics-12)[[7]](#cite_note-picturegoer-7)

Simmons was top-billed for the first time in the drama *[Uncle Silas](/wiki/Uncle_Silas_(film) "Uncle Silas (film)")* (1947). She followed it with *[The Woman in the Hall](/wiki/The_Woman_in_the_Hall "The Woman in the Hall")* (1947). Neither was particularly successful, but Simmons was then in a huge international hit, playing [Ophelia](/wiki/Ophelia "Ophelia") in [Laurence Olivier](/wiki/Laurence_Olivier "Laurence Olivier")'s *[Hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet_(1948_film) "Hamlet (1948 film)")* (1948), for which she received her first Oscar nomination. Olivier offered her the chance to work and study at the [Old Vic](/wiki/Old_Vic "Old Vic"), advising her to play anything they offered her to get experience, but she was under contract to the J. Arthur Rank Organisation, which vetoed the idea.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Simmons had the lead in [Frank Launder](/wiki/Frank_Launder "Frank Launder")'s *[The Blue Lagoon](/wiki/The_Blue_Lagoon_(1949_film) "The Blue Lagoon (1949 film)")* (1949), based on [the 1908 novel](/wiki/The_Blue_Lagoon_(novel) "The Blue Lagoon (novel)") by [Henry De Vere Stacpoole](/wiki/Henry_De_Vere_Stacpoole "Henry De Vere Stacpoole") and co-produced with Launder's partner [Sidney Gilliat](/wiki/Sidney_Gilliat "Sidney Gilliat"),[[14]](#cite_note-14) a project originally announced for Lockwood a decade earlier. It was a considerable financial success.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Simmons starred with Stewart Granger in the comedy *[Adam and Evelyne](/wiki/Adam_and_Evelyne "Adam and Evelyne")* (1949). It was her first adult role, and Granger and she became romantically involved; they soon married.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Simmons made two films that were popular at the local box office: *[So Long at the Fair](/wiki/So_Long_at_the_Fair "So Long at the Fair")* (1950) with [Dirk Bogarde](/wiki/Dirk_Bogarde "Dirk Bogarde") and *[Trio](/wiki/Trio_(1950_film) "Trio (1950 film)")* (1950), where she was one of several stars. She was then in *[Cage of Gold](/wiki/Cage_of_Gold "Cage of Gold")* (1950) with [David Farrar](/wiki/David_Farrar_(actor) "David Farrar (actor)") and [Ralph Thomas](/wiki/Ralph_Thomas "Ralph Thomas")' *[The Clouded Yellow](/wiki/The_Clouded_Yellow "The Clouded Yellow")* (1950) with [Trevor Howard](/wiki/Trevor_Howard "Trevor Howard"). In 1950, Simmons was voted the fourth-most popular star in Britain.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

### Hollywood

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Simmons&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Hollywood")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Victor_Mature_and_Jean_Simmons_in_Androcles_and_the_Lion%2C_1952.jpg/250px-Victor_Mature_and_Jean_Simmons_in_Androcles_and_the_Lion%2C_1952.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victor_Mature_and_Jean_Simmons_in_Androcles_and_the_Lion,_1952.jpg)

Simmons with [Victor Mature](/wiki/Victor_Mature "Victor Mature") in *[Androcles and the Lion](/wiki/Androcles_and_the_Lion_(1952_film) "Androcles and the Lion (1952 film)")* (1952)

Granger became a Hollywood star in *[King Solomon's Mines](/wiki/King_Solomon%27s_Mines_(1950_film) "King Solomon's Mines (1950 film)")* (1950) and was signed to a contract by MGM, so Simmons moved to Los Angeles with him. In 1951, Rank sold her contract to [Howard Hughes](/wiki/Howard_Hughes "Howard Hughes"), who then owned [RKO Pictures](/wiki/RKO_Pictures "RKO Pictures").[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Hughes was eager to start a sexual relationship with Simmons, but Granger put a stop to his advances by angrily telling Hughes over the phone: "Mr. Howard bloody Hughes, you'll be sorry if you don't leave my wife alone."[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Her first Hollywood film was *Androcles and the Lion* (1952), produced by Pascal and co-starring Victor Mature. It was followed by *[Angel Face](/wiki/Angel_Face_(1953_film) "Angel Face (1953 film)")* (1953), directed by [Otto Preminger](/wiki/Otto_Preminger "Otto Preminger") with [Robert Mitchum](/wiki/Robert_Mitchum "Robert Mitchum"). [David Thomson](/wiki/David_Thomson_(film_critic) "David Thomson (film critic)") wrote that "if she had made only one film – *Angel Face* – she might now be spoken of with the awe given to [Louise Brooks](/wiki/Louise_Brooks "Louise Brooks")."[[21]](#cite_note-Thomson-21) Smarting over his rebuff from Granger, Hughes instructed Preminger to treat Simmons as roughly as possible, leading the director to demand that costar Mitchum repeatedly slap the actress harder and harder, until Mitchum turned and punched Preminger, asking if that was how he wanted it.[[22]](#cite_note-22) He also made her appear in *[She Couldn't Say No](/wiki/She_Couldn%27t_Say_No_(1954_film) "She Couldn't Say No (1954 film)")* (1954), a comedy with Mitchum.

A court case freed Simmons from the contract with Hughes in 1952.[[21]](#cite_note-Thomson-21) They settled out of court; part of the arrangement was that Simmons would do one more film for no additional money.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Simmons also agreed to make three more movies under the auspices of RKO, but not actually at that studio—she would be lent out. She would make an additional picture for 20th Century Fox while RKO got the services of Victor Mature for one film.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

Simmons and Granger returned to England to make the thriller *[Footsteps in the Fog](/wiki/Footsteps_in_the_Fog "Footsteps in the Fog")* (1955). Then, [Joseph Mankiewicz](/wiki/Joseph_Mankiewicz "Joseph Mankiewicz") cast her opposite [Marlon Brando](/wiki/Marlon_Brando "Marlon Brando") in the screen adaptation of *Guys and Dolls* (1955), where she did her own singing in a role turned down by [Grace Kelly](/wiki/Grace_Kelly "Grace Kelly"); it was a big hit.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

So, too, were *[This Could Be the Night](/wiki/This_Could_Be_the_Night_(film) "This Could Be the Night (film)")* (1957) and *[Until They Sail](/wiki/Until_They_Sail "Until They Sail")* (1957), both at MGM.

*[The Big Country](/wiki/The_Big_Country "The Big Country")* (1958), directed by [William Wyler](/wiki/William_Wyler "William Wyler"), was a great success for Simmons. She starred in *[Home Before Dark](/wiki/Home_Before_Dark_(film) "Home Before Dark (film)")* (1958) at Warner Bros. and *[This Earth Is Mine](/wiki/This_Earth_Is_Mine_(1959_film) "This Earth Is Mine (1959 film)")* (1959) with [Rock Hudson](/wiki/Rock_Hudson "Rock Hudson") at Universal. In the opinion of film critic [Philip French](/wiki/Philip_French "Philip French"), *Home Before Dark* was "perhaps her finest performance as a housewife driven into a breakdown in [Mervyn LeRoy](/wiki/Mervyn_LeRoy "Mervyn LeRoy")'s psychodrama."[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Simmons went into *[Elmer Gantry](/wiki/Elmer_Gantry_(film) "Elmer Gantry (film)")* (1960), directed by [Richard Brooks](/wiki/Richard_Brooks "Richard Brooks"), who became her second husband. It was successful, as was *[Spartacus](/wiki/Spartacus_(film) "Spartacus (film)")* (1960), where she played [Kirk Douglas](/wiki/Kirk_Douglas "Kirk Douglas")'s character's love interest. Simmons then did *[The Grass Is Greener](/wiki/The_Grass_Is_Greener "The Grass Is Greener")* (1960) with Mitchum, [Cary Grant](/wiki/Cary_Grant "Cary Grant"), and [Deborah Kerr](/wiki/Deborah_Kerr "Deborah Kerr").

She took some years off screen, then returned in *[All the Way Home](/wiki/All_the_Way_Home_(1963_film) "All the Way Home (1963 film)")* (1963) with [Robert Preston](/wiki/Robert_Preston_(actor) "Robert Preston (actor)"). She did *[Life at the Top](/wiki/Life_at_the_Top_(film) "Life at the Top (film)")* (1965) with [Laurence Harvey](/wiki/Laurence_Harvey "Laurence Harvey"), *[Mister Buddwing](/wiki/Mister_Buddwing "Mister Buddwing")* (1966) with [James Garner](/wiki/James_Garner "James Garner"), *[Divorce American Style](/wiki/Divorce_American_Style "Divorce American Style")* (1967) with [Dick Van Dyke](/wiki/Dick_Van_Dyke "Dick Van Dyke"), and *[Rough Night in Jericho](/wiki/Rough_Night_in_Jericho_(film) "Rough Night in Jericho (film)")* (1967) with [George Peppard](/wiki/George_Peppard "George Peppard") and Dean Martin.

### 1970s and 1980s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Simmons&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 1970s and 1980s")]

By the 1970s, Simmons turned her focus to stage and television acting. She toured the United States in [Stephen Sondheim](/wiki/Stephen_Sondheim "Stephen Sondheim")'s *[A Little Night Music](/wiki/A_Little_Night_Music "A Little Night Music")*, then took the show to London, thus originating the role of Desirée Armfeldt in the [West End](/wiki/West_End_theatre "West End theatre"). Performing in the show for three years, she said she never tired of Sondheim's music; "No matter how tired or 'off' you felt, the music would just pick you up."[[27]](#cite_note-27)

She portrayed Fiona "Fee" Cleary, the Cleary family matriarch, in the miniseries *The Thorn Birds* (1983); she won an [Emmy Award](/wiki/Emmy_Award "Emmy Award") for her role. She appeared in *[North and South](/wiki/North_and_South_(miniseries) "North and South (miniseries)")* (1985–86), again playing the role of the family matriarch as Clarissa Main, and starred in *[The Dawning](/wiki/The_Dawning "The Dawning")* (1988) with [Anthony Hopkins](/wiki/Anthony_Hopkins "Anthony Hopkins") and [Hugh Grant](/wiki/Hugh_Grant "Hugh Grant"). In 1989, Simmons appeared as murder mystery author Eudora McVeigh Shipton, a self-proclaimed rival to Jessica Fletcher, in the two-part [Murder, She Wrote](/wiki/Murder,_She_Wrote "Murder, She Wrote") episode "Mirror, Mirror, On the Wall" with [Angela Lansbury](/wiki/Angela_Lansbury "Angela Lansbury").

### 1990s and 2000s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Simmons&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 1990s and 2000s")]

In 1989, she starred in a remake of *[Great Expectations](/wiki/Great_Expectations_(1989_TV_series) "Great Expectations (1989 TV series)")*, this time playing the role of Miss Havisham, Estella's adoptive mother. In 1991, she appeared in the *[Star Trek: The Next Generation](/wiki/Star_Trek:_The_Next_Generation "Star Trek: The Next Generation")* episode "[The Drumhead](/wiki/The_Drumhead "The Drumhead")" as a retired [Starfleet](/wiki/Starfleet "Starfleet") admiral and hardened legal investigator who conducts a [witch hunt](/wiki/Witch-hunt#Metaphorical_usage "Witch-hunt"); and as Elizabeth Collins Stoddard/Naomi Collins, in the short-lived revival of the 1960s daytime series [*Dark Shadows*](/wiki/Dark_Shadows_(1991_TV_series) "Dark Shadows (1991 TV series)"), in roles originally played by [Joan Bennett](/wiki/Joan_Bennett "Joan Bennett"). From 1994 until 1998, Simmons narrated the [A&E](/wiki/A%26E_Network "A&E Network") documentary television series *[Mysteries of the Bible](/wiki/Mysteries_of_the_Bible "Mysteries of the Bible")*. In 1995, she appeared in *[How to Make an American Quilt](/wiki/How_to_Make_an_American_Quilt "How to Make an American Quilt")* with [Winona Ryder](/wiki/Winona_Ryder "Winona Ryder"), [Maya Angelou](/wiki/Maya_Angelou "Maya Angelou"), [Ellen Burstyn](/wiki/Ellen_Burstyn "Ellen Burstyn"), [Anne Bancroft](/wiki/Anne_Bancroft "Anne Bancroft"), and [Alfre Woodard](/wiki/Alfre_Woodard "Alfre Woodard"). In 2004, she voiced the lead role of Sophie in the English dub of *[Howl's Moving Castle](/wiki/Howl%27s_Moving_Castle_(film) "Howl's Moving Castle (film)")*.[[12]](#cite_note-reelclassics-12)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Simmons&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Personal life")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/66/Stewart_Granger_and_Jean_Simmons%2C_1955.jpg/250px-Stewart_Granger_and_Jean_Simmons%2C_1955.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stewart_Granger_and_Jean_Simmons,_1955.jpg)

Simmons with her first husband [Stewart Granger](/wiki/Stewart_Granger "Stewart Granger") in 1955

Simmons was married and divorced twice. At 21, she married [Stewart Granger](/wiki/Stewart_Granger "Stewart Granger") in Tucson, Arizona, on 20 December 1950.[[28]](#cite_note-28) She and Granger became US citizens in 1956;[[29]](#cite_note-29) in the same year, their daughter Tracy Granger was born. They divorced in 1960.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

On 1 November 1960, Simmons married director [Richard Brooks](/wiki/Richard_Brooks "Richard Brooks");[[31]](#cite_note-31) their daughter, Kate Brooks, was born a year later. Simmons and Brooks divorced in 1980.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDaniel2011210-32) Although both men were significantly older than Simmons, she denied that she was looking for a father figure. Her father had died when she was just 16, but she said:

> They were really nothing like my father at all. My father was a gentle, softly spoken man. My husbands were both much noisier and much more opinionated ... it's really nothing to do with age ... it's to do with what's there – the twinkle and sense of humour.[[11]](#cite_note-weekly-11)

In a 1984 interview, given in Copenhagen at the time she was shooting the film *Going Undercover* (1988,[[33]](#cite_note-BFIGU-33)[[34]](#cite_note-LAT1988-34) a.k.a. *Yellow Pages*; completed 1985)[[35]](#cite_note-BBFCYP-35) she elaborated slightly on her marriages, stating,

> It may be simplistic, but you could sum up my two marriages by saying that, when I wanted to be a wife, Jimmy [Stewart Granger] would say: "I just want you to be pretty." And when I wanted to cook, Richard would say: "Forget the cooking. You've been trained to act – so act!" Most people thought I was quite helpless – a clinger and a butterfly – during my first marriage. It was Richard Brooks who saw what was wrong and tried to make me stand on my own two feet. I'd whine: 'I'm afraid.' And he'd say: 'Never be afraid to fail. Every time you get up in the morning, you are ahead.'

Simmons had two daughters, Tracy Granger and Kate Brooks, one by each marriage. Tracy's name reflects her friendship with [Spencer Tracy](/wiki/Spencer_Tracy "Spencer Tracy");[[36]](#cite_note-36) Tracy became a film editor, and Kate a TV production assistant and producer.

In the [2003 New Year Honours](/wiki/2003_New_Year_Honours "2003 New Year Honours"), Simmons was appointed an [Officer of the Order of the British Empire](/wiki/Officer_of_the_Order_of_the_British_Empire "Officer of the Order of the British Empire") (OBE) for services to acting.[[37]](#cite_note-LG_31_December_2002-37)

In 2003, she became the patron of the British drugs and human rights charity [Release](/wiki/Release_(agency) "Release (agency)"). In 2005, she signed a petition to British Prime Minister [Tony Blair](/wiki/Tony_Blair "Tony Blair") asking him not to upgrade [cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)") from a class C drug to class B.[[38]](#cite_note-petition-38)

## Illness and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Simmons&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Illness and death")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/Grave_of_Jean_Simmons.jpg/250px-Grave_of_Jean_Simmons.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grave_of_Jean_Simmons.jpg)

Grave of Jean Simmons in [Highgate Cemetery](/wiki/Highgate_Cemetery "Highgate Cemetery"), London

Simmons, aged 80, died from lung cancer at her home in Santa Monica on 22 January 2010. She was interred in [Highgate Cemetery](/wiki/Highgate_Cemetery "Highgate Cemetery"), north London.[[39]](#cite_note-death-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

## Box office ranking

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jean_Simmons&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Box office ranking")]

For a number of years, British film exhibitors voted Simmons among the top ten British stars at the box office via an annual poll in the *Motion Picture Herald*.

* 1949 – 4th[[45]](#cite_note-45) (9th most popular overall)[[46]](#cite_note-46)
* 1950 – 2nd (4th most popular overall)[[47]](#cite_note-47)
* 1951 – 3rd[[48]](#cite_note-48)
